Facts.text: First War of the Guelderian Succession ends when Matilda and the Heeckerens are defeated by the Duchy of Jülich, forcing her to resign her rights to the Duchy of Guelders to her half-sister Maria's son, William
German Parliament opens in Worms
France and England sign the Peace of Boulogne
First game law is passed in the American colonies by Virginia
Jerome (aka Hieronimus) de Salis naturalised as British by Act of Parliamentary.
Britain enacts Quartering Act, required colonists to provide temporary housing to British soldiers
Benjamin West (US) becomes president of Royal Academy of London
Handel & Haydn Society of Boston founded
Philadelphia & Columbia Railway is authorized, the first state owned railway
Canada grants its Black citizens the right to vote
State of siege proclaimed in Amsterdam
Manhattan Kansas founded as New Boston Kansas
Clipper Andrew Jackson arrives in San Francisco, 89 days out of New York
Metropolitan Life Insurance Co forms
Ciro Pinsuti's final opera "Mattia Corvino" first performance at the Teatro alla Scala, in Milan, Italy
University boat race between Oxford & Cambridge ends in a dead heat
British frigate Eurydice sunk; 300 lost
Tobacco Growers' Mutual Insurance Company incorporates in CT
1st telephone call between NY & Chicago
Oscar Straus appointed 1st Jewish ambassador from US (to Turkey)
37 miners killed at Franklin, Washington
New York City Mayor Robert Anderson Van Wyck breaks ground for a new underground "Rapid Transit Railroad" that would link Manhattan and Brooklyn.
Census of the British Empire shows the United Kingdom rules one-fifth of the world's landmass
The first issue of the Georgian Bolshevik newspaper Dro is published
83°F highest temperature ever recorded in Cleveland in March
Dutch soccer forward Huug de Groot scores twice as Netherlands score first ever victory over England; 2-1 at HBS, The Hague
Palace Theater opens at 1564 Broadway, New York City
German submarines torpedo the unarmed French cross-channel packet 'Sussex'
1st US coast guard air station established (Morehead City, NC)
Greece becomes a republic
KSL-AM in Salt Lake City UT begins radio transmissions
The Beehive in the Hague opens 1st escalator in Netherlands
Dutch 1st Chamber condemns Belgian and Netherlands Wielingen Treaty
1st religious services telecast in US (W2XBS NYC)
1st US radio broadcast from a moving train (Belle Baker WABC from MD)
Peter I Island incorporated as a Norwegian dependency
U.S. Congress passes the Tydings-McDuffie Act, declaring the Philippines independent after a period of 10 years
Major Bowes' Original Amateur Hour goes national on NBC Radio Network
Detroit Red Wings beat Montreal Maroons, 1-0 in 16:30 of 6th period of OT; record Stanley Cup playoff game lasts 9 periods (176 minutes)
Bus carrying a roller skater troupe to Cincinnati from St.Louis crashes into a bridge abutment in Salem, Illinois, killing 21 people
National Gallery of Art established by Congress
British troops defeat Italians in Somaliland
German troops occupy El Agheila Libya
Richard Wright and Paul Green's play "Native Son" premieres in NYC
US government begins moving native-born citizens with Japanese ancestry into detention centres under Executive Order 9066, with intention of preventing home-grown espionage
76 Allied officers escape Stalag Luft 3 (Great Escape)
811 British bombers attack Berlin

RAF rear gunner Nicholas Alkemade survives a jump from his Lancaster bomber 18,000 feet over Germany without a parachute; his fall is broken by pine trees and soft snow, and he suffers only a sprained leg
Operation Varsity: In the largest one-day airborne operation of all time, British, US & Canadian paratroopers land east of the Rhine in Northern Germany
US minesweepers reach Kerama Retto, South coast of Okinawa
US Congress proposes 2-term limitation on the presidency
21st Academy Awards: "Hamlet",Laurence Olivier& Jane Wyman win, Walter & John Huston become 1st father-and-son team to win awards
SS police chief in the Netherlands Hanns Albin Rauter's request for a pardon denied, executed by firing squad
Great demonstrations against apartheid in South Africa
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
1st seagoing oil drill rig placed in service
British Army patrols withdraw from Belfast after 20 years
Iraq withdraws from the Baghdad Pact
NY Senate approves $55M for a baseball stadium at Flushing Meadows
Kennedy half-dollar issued
US Ranger 9 strikes Moon, 10 miles (16 km) NE of crater Alphonsus
Selective Service announces college deferments based on performance
University of Michigan holds 1st "Teach-in" after bombing of North Vietnam
Francis Turner is sworn in as the director of the Federal Highway Administration
Dutch cartoonist Frans Piet ends "Sjors & Sjimmie" strip
Serge Gainsbourg releases his concept album "Histoire de Melody Nelson"
Great Britain imposes direct rule over Northern Ireland
Professional track debut of Kip Keino defeating Jim Ryun in the mile
San Francisco 49er president Lou Spadia proposes the NFL expands to 30 teams
Wings release single  "With a Little Luck"
Columbia flown on carrier aircraft lands at Kennedy Space Center
"Nightline with Ted Koppel" premieres on ABC
Capitol Records releases some rare Beatles tracks on the LP "Rarites"
Bombay beat Delhi by innings & 46 to win Ranji Cricket Trophy
Colombia drops diplomatic relations with Cuba
US sub Jacksonville collides with a Turkish freighter near Virginia
Andrea Schone skates ladies world record 5 km (7:34.52)
Igor Malkov skates world record 10 km (14:21.51)
IOC agrees to 6-team exhibition baseball tournament in Olympics
Philip Glass' opera "Akhnaten", based on the life and religious convictions of the Egyptian pharaoh, premieres at the Staatstheater, in Stuttgart, Germany
5th Golden Raspberry (Razzie) Awards: "Bolero" wins
Norman Gifford makes cricket ODI debuts at age 44 (v Aust, Sharjah)
NASA publishes "Strategy for Safely Returning the Space Shuttle to Flight Status"
Suriname army capt Etienne Boerenveen arrested for cocaine smuggling
US & Libya clash in Gulf of Sidra
San Diego Chargers quarterback Dan Fouts announces his retirement after 15 seasons
Exxon tanker Valdez spills 10.8 million gallons of crude oil in Prince William Sound, near Tatitlek, Alaska
Indian troops leave Sri Lanka
Tom Hunter swims world record 50m freestyle (21.81 sec)
Banks reopen in liberated Kuwait
Barcelona Dragons beat NY/NJ Knights 19-7 in their 1st WLAF game
NY Yankees beat NY Mets, 9-3
Space Shuttle STS-45 (Atlantis 11) launches into space with the first Belgian astronaut on board, Dirk Frimout
Sudanese Boeing 707 crashes on mountain Hymettos at Athens: 5-6 die
Ezer Weizman elected president of Israel
South African President F.W. Willem de Klerk announces to South Africa's Parliament the existence and abandonment of the former nuclear weapons program
MTA raises NYC bridge tolls to $3.50 each way
Australian parliament overturns world's 1st & only euthanasia law
A tornado sweeps through Dantan in India killing 250 people and injuring 3000 others.
Jonesboro massacre: Two students, ages 11 and 13, fire upon teachers and students at Westside Middle School in Jonesboro, Arkansas; five people are dead and ten are wounded.
Mont Blanc Tunnel Fire: 39 people die when a Belgian transport truck carrying flour and margarine caught fire in the Mont Blanc Tunnel.
NATO commences an air bombardment against Yugoslavia during the Kosovo War, the first attack by NATO on a sovereign country
The Arab League votes 21-1 in favor of a resolution demanding the immediate and unconditional removal of U.S. and British soldiers from Iraq.
Long-term protests in Belarus are broken by police.
The Australian Labor Party is reinstated after the New South Wales state elections.
Bhutan officially becomes a democracy, with its first ever general election.
Lee Kun-hee returns to Samsung Electronics chief executive officer (CEO) position after his resignation in April 21, 2008
African Union deploys 5,000 strong force with the aim of catching or killing warlord Joseph Kony
17 soldiers are killed by a suicide bomber at a military checkpoint in North Waziristan, Pakistan
25 people are killed by gunmen in a coordinated attack in Adamawa State, Nigeria
A series of emergency meetings in Brussels undertaken to resolve Cyprus’ financial situation
Scotland defeats Sweden to win the 2013 World Women's Curling Championship
Ukraine withdraws its forces from the Crimea
Germanwings flight on route between Barcelona and Düsseldorf crashes in the French Alps killing all 150 on board
Former English international footballer Adam Johnson sentenced to 6 years in prison for sexual activity with a schoolgirl
American conducts its first ever drone strike against Al-Qaeda militants in southern Libya
Australian batsman Cameron Bancroft is caught on camera rubbing the match ball with an object during the third cricket test in Cape Town, resulting in an infamous ball-tampering scandal
Hundreds of protesters shut down special meeting at Sacramento City Hall investigating police shooting of Stephon Clark
Tens of thousands attend 'March for Our Lives' rallies held in Washington, D.C. and around the world to protest gun violence
2-time American League MVP Mike Trout signs the biggest contract in North American sports history, a $426.5 million, 12-year extension with the Los Angeles Angels

US Attorney William Barr summarizes report submitted by Special Council Robert S. Mueller (The Mueller Report) in a letter to Congress stating "investigation did not establish that members of the Trump Campaign conspired or coordinated with the Russian government in its election interference activities" and "Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein and I have concluded that the evidence developed during the Special Counsel's investigation is not sufficient to establish that the President committed an obstruction-of-justice offense"
China's Hubei province, the original center of the COVID-19 outbreak, eases restrictions on travel after a nearly two-month lockdown
Africa's first centralised biobank -  Biodiversity Biobanks South Africa, collecting more than a million biodiversity samples opens in Pretoria, South Africa
Chocolate factory explosion in West Reading Borough, Pennsylvania, kills at least four people and injures 10, with three missing
Supercell storm produces powerful EF-4 tornado in the Mississippi Delta, which stays on the ground for more than an hour and over 170 miles, killing 26 people
Brazilian police arrest two politicians and a police chief for the notorious murder and its cover-up of politician and activist Marielle Franco in 2018
Senegal presidential election won by opposition figure Bassirou Diomaye Faye, only 10 days after he was released from prison

"The Atlantic" publishes "The Trump Administration Accidentally Texted Me Its War Plans" claiming US Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth included a journalist in a group chat detailing airstrikes in Yemen
Timurattacks city of Damascus, second city of the Mameluke Empire. Though scholar and negotiatorIbn Khaldūn's life spared, the city is sacked and the Umayyad Mosque destroyed.
Tokugawa Ieyasuis granted the title of shogun, officially establishing the Tokugawa Shogunate which would rule Japan until 1867
Johann Sebastian Bachdedicates his Brandenburg Concertos to Christian Ludwig, Margrave of Brandenburg-Schwedt
Mormon leaderJoseph Smithis beaten, tarred and feathered in Hiram, Ohio
Anti-slavery newspaper "The Provincial Freeman" first published in Windsor, Ontario, edited by Samuel Ringgold Ward andMary Ann Shadd Cary, first black woman publisher in North America
German scientistRobert Kochdiscovers and describes the tubercle bacillus, which causes tuberculosis (Mycobacterium tuberculosis), establishing germ theory
A group of Cretans dedicated to Crete's union with Greece led by EleutheriosVenizelos, meet at the village of Therisso and proclaim a union in defiance of the Great Powers
1st performance ofJean Sibelius' 7th Symphony in C at the Konsertföreningenin in Stockholm
Dmitri Shostakovich's Suite for Jazz Orchestra No. 1 premieres in Leningrad, USSR
"Wuthering Heights" film based on theEmily Brontënovel, directed byWilliam Wylerand starring Merle Oberon andLaurence Olivier, premieres in Los Angeles
Glenn Millerbegins work on his 1st movie "Sun Valley Serenade", starringSonja Henieand John Payne, for 20th Century Fox

Ardeatine massacre: Nazis led by SS officers Herbert Kappler,Erich Priebkeand Karl Hass execute 335 civilians and political prisoners in occupied Rome in retaliation for the previous day's Via Rassela bombing that killed 33 Germans
Allied generalsDwight D. Eisenhower,Bernard MontgomeryandOmar Bradleydiscuss advance in Germany
John D. Rockefeller Jr. donates NYC East River site to the UN
The Party of the African Federation (PFA) is launched byLéopold Sédar Senghorand Modibo Keita.
Emile Griffithbeats Benny " Kid" Paret by TKO in 12th round in welterweight boxing title fight at MSG, NYC; Paret dies 10 days later; first use of television slow motion replay
Mick Jagger&Keith Richardsperform as Little Boy Blue & Blue Boys
Muhammad AliTKOs Chuck Wepner in 15 for heavyweight boxing title
New Orleans R&B piano player Professor Longhair with the Meters perform at record release party forPaul McCartneyand Wings' "Venus and Mars", aboard the Queen Mary in Long Beach, California
Argentine President Isabel Martínez de Perón is deposed in a military coup headed byJorge Rafael Videla
Lily Tomlinis the first woman to appear solo in a Broadway show in "Appearing Nitely" (special Tony award 1977)
Michigan State'sEarvin"Magic" Johnson registers a triple-double with 29 points, 10 rebounds, and 10 assists as the Spartans cruise to a 101-67 victory over Penn State
Deposed Shah of IranMohammad Reza Pahlavi(60) arrives in Egypt for cancer treatment at Maadi Military Hospital, near Cairo
Third straight WTA Tour Championship for tennis legendMartina Navratilova; beats Helena Suková 6–3, 7–5, 6–4 at Madison Square Garden, NYC
58th Academy Awards: "Out of Africa",William Hurt&Geraldine Pagewin

Men's International Professional Tennis Council announcesJimmy Connors' 10 week suspension and $20,000 fine for storming off the court after defaulting againstIvan Lendlat Boca Raton, Florida in February
NBC rebroadcastsMary Martinand Cyril Ritchard's 1960 version of "Peter Pan" for the first time since 1973
Ayrton Sennawins the Brazilian Grand Prix for the 1st time, at Interlagos, São Paulo
69th Academy Awards: "The English Patient",Geoffrey RushandFrances McDormandwin
"The Matrix" film written and directed by The Wachowskis, starring Keanu Reeve, Lawrence Fishburne and Carrie-Ann Moss premieres
74th Academy Awards: "A Beautiful Mind",Denzel WashingtonandHalle Berrywin; Berry is the first woman of colour to win the Best Actress award
US version of "The Office" created byRicky Gervaisand Stephen Merchant, developed by Greg Daniels, starringSteve CarellandJohn Krasinskipremieres on NBC
PopeBenedict XVIadds 15 men to the College of Cardinals, in the first consistory of his Pontificate
The World Health Organization appoints British singerCraig Davidas a Goodwill Ambassador against tuberculosis on World Tuberculosis Day
Bosnian Serb politicianRadovan Karadžićis found guilty of genocide, crimes against humanity and war crimes during the 1995 Srebrenica massacre and sentenced to 40 years imprisonment
US PresidentDonald Trumpand Republican party forced to pull their attempt to repeal Affordable Care Act ('Obamacare') after internal opposition
Margo Hayescompletes her third 9a+ (5.15a) graded climb, Papichulo at Oliana, Spain, becoming the first woman to complete the '9a+ Trilogy'
New England Patriots tight endRob Gronkowskiannounces retirement as 3-time Super Bowl winner, tight end postseason record, most receptions (81), receiving yards (1,163) and receiving TDs (12)
Indian PMNarendra Modiorders a 21-day lockdown for the country's 1.3 billion people to address COVID-19
Japan's Prime MinisterShinzō Abeannounces the postponement of the Tokyo 2020 Olympic and Paralympic Games to the summer of 2021 due to the global COVID-19 pandemic
Scottish KingJames VI, son of Mary, Queen of Scots, becomesKing James Iof England in succession to Elizabeth I, uniting the English and Scottish crowns
Elvis Presleyjoins the U.S. Army (serial number 53310761)
ABC's nightly Iran Hostage crisis program renamed "Nightline"
NASA's Opportunity rover becomes the first to complete a Martian marathon
K'inich Janaab' Pakal [Pacal the Great], Ajaw (King) of Palenque (615-83), born in Palenque (d. 683)
Ferrand, Count of Flanders, son of Sancho I, born in Coimbra, Portugal (d.  1223)
Ernst I, Elector of Saxony (1464-86), born in Meissen (d. 1486)
Georgius Agricola, German mineralogist (De Re Metallica), born in Glauchau, Saxony (d. 1555)
Michiel de Ruyter, Dutch admiral (famous victories during Anglo-Dutch Wars), born in Vlissingen, Spanish Netherlands (d. 1676)
Sophie Amalie of Brunswick-Calenberg, Queen of Denmark and Norway by marriage to King Frederick III, born in Herzberg Castle, Lower Saxony (d. 1685)
José Saenz d'Aguirre, Spanish cardinal, born in Logro, Old Castile (d. 1699)
Georg Philipp Telemann, German late Baroque composer, and multi-instrumentalist, born in Magdeburg, Margraviate of Brandenburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1767)
Jose Francisco de Isla [Francisco de Salazar], Spanish Jesuit preacher and satirist writer, born in Vidanes, Spain (d. 1781)
Carlo Giovanni Testori, Italian violinist, composer, pedagogue, and musicologist (La musica raggionata), born in Vercelli, Kingdom of Venice (d. 1782)
Samuel Ashe, American 9th Governor of North Carolin (1795-8), born in Beaufort, Province of North Carolina (d. 1813)
Thomas Cushing, American statesman, Continental Congressman, acting Governor of Massachusetts (1785) and dangerous radical, born in Boston, Province of Massachusetts Bay (d. 1788)
Gian Francesco de Majo, Italian composer, born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples, House of Habsburg (d. 1770)
John Antes, American composer and missionary, born in Frederick, Pennsylvania (d. 1811)
Bernard Jumentier, French composer, primarily of sacred music, born in Lèves, France (d. 1829)
Joel Barlow, American diplomat, author and poet (Advice to the Privileged Orders, The Hasty Pudding), born in Redding, Connecticut (d. 1812)
Rufus King, American politician and framer of US constitution, born in Scarborough, Massachusetts (d. 1827)

Marcos Portugal [Marcos Antonio da Fonseca Portugal], Portuguese-Brazilian opera, sacred music, and national anthem composer (Portugal - "Hymno Patriótico", 1809; Brazil - "Hino da Independência", 1822), born in Portugal (d. 1830)
Pierre-Louis Hus-Desforges, French composer and cellist, born in Toulon, France (d. 1838)
Orest Kiprensky, leading Russian portrait painter in the Age of Romanticism, born in Nezhnovo, Russian Empire (d. 1836)
John Corry Wilson Daly, English-Canadian politician and 1st Mayor of Stratford, Ontario, born in Liverpool, England (d. 1878)
Antonio Rosmini-Serbati, Italian philosopher and priest (founder Institute of Charity), born in Rovereto, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1855)
Jacob van Lennep, Dutch poet and novelist (The Adopted Son), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1868)
Maria Malibran (née Garcia) Spanish operatic contralto and soprano, born in Paris, France (d. 1836)
Joseph Liouville, French mathematician (discover of transcendental numbers), born in Saint-Omer, France (d. 1882)
Mariano José de Larra, Spanish journalist and romantic writer, born in Madrid, Spain (d. 1837)
Aimé Maillart, French opera composer (Les Dragons de Villars), born in Montpellier, Hérault, France (d. 1871)
Edmond Becquerel, French physicist who studied the solar spectrum, magnetism, electricity and optics (photovoltaic effect), born in Paris, France (d. 1891)
Fanny Crosby, American hymnist known as the "Queen of Gospel Song Writers", born in Brewster, New York (d. 1915)
Rachel Félix, Swiss-born French actress in tragedies (mistress of Napoleon III), born in Mumpf, Switzerland (d. 1858)
[George] Hector Tyndale, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1880)
George Francis Train, American businessman and organizer of the Union Pacific Railroad, born in Boston (d. 1904)
Ignacio Zaragoza, Mexican Army officer and politician, born in Presidio La Bahía, Mexican Republic (d. 1862)
Robert Hamerling, Austrian poet known for his epics (Ahasuerus in Rome, Homumculus), born in Kirchberg am Walde, Austrian Empire (d. 1889)[1]
John Wesley Powell, American geologist, explorer (Grand Canyon) and ethnologist, born in Mount Morris, New York (d. 1902)
William Morris, English arts and Crafts designer, craftsman (championed handmade production), poet and socialist, born in Walthamstow, London, England (d. 1896)[1]
Josef Stefan, Slovenian-Austrian physicist (Stefan-Boltzmann law), born in St. Peter, Austria (d. 1893)
Prince Philippe, Count of Flanders and son of Leopold I, born in Royal Castle of Laeken, Belgium (d. 1905)
Augustine Sackett, American inventor of drywall, born in Connecticut (d. 1814)[1]
Honoré Beaugrand, French Canadian journalist, newspaper publisher (La Patrie), and politician (Mayor of Montreal, 1885-87), born in Saint-Joseph-de-Lanoraie, Quebec, Canada East (d. 1906)
Silas K. Hocking, British novelist and Methodist preacher (Her Benny), born in Cornwall, England (d. 1935)
Olive Schreiner, South African writer (Portrait of a South African Woman), born in Wittebergen, Eastern Cape (d. 1920)
Jack McAuliffe, Irish-American boxer and undefeated World Lightweight champion (1886-93), born in Cork, Ireland, United Kingdom (d. 1937)
Leslie Gay, English cricket wicket-keeper (1 Test; Cambridge Uni, Hampshire CCC) and soccer goalkeeper (3 caps; Cambridge Uni), born in Brighton, Sussex (d. 1949)
Luigi Einaudi, Italian economist and politician (1st president of Italy 1948-55), born in Carrù, Italy (d. 1961)
Top Naeff, Dutch writer (School Idylls), born in Dordrecht, Netherlands (d. 1953)
James I Wedgwood, British theosophist and bishop (Liberal Catholic Church), born in London (d. 1951)
Gino Marinuzzi, Sr., Italian opera conductor (Wagner; Puccini), and composer (Symphony in A), born in Palermo, Sicily, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1945)
Peter Debye, Dutch-American physical chemist (Nobel Prize for Chemistry 1936 for investigations of dipole moments, X-rays, and light scattering in gases), born in Maastricht, Netherlands (d. 1966)
Charlie Daniels, American swimmer (Olympic gold 220yd, 440yd, 4x50yd freestyle 1904, 100m freestyle 1908), born in Dayton, Ohio (d. 1973)
Edward Weston, American photographer considered one of the most influential American photographers, born in Chicago (d. 1958)
Roscoe "Fatty" Arbuckle, American actor (The Bell Boy, Moonshine, Keystone comedies), born in Smith Center, Kansas (d. 1933)
Jameson Thomas, British actor (Piccadilly, Farmer's Wife), born in London, England (d. 1939)
Viktor Kingissepp, Estonian politician and leader of the Estonian Communist Party, born in Kaarma-Suure, Russian Empire (d. 1922)[1][2]
Albert Hill, English athlete (Olympic gold 800m, 1500m 1920), born in London (d. 1969)
Agnes Macphail, Canadian politician (first women elected to Canada's House of Commons), born in Proton Township, Ontario (d. 1954)[1]
John Rock, American obstetrician and gynecologist who co-developed the 1st birth control pill, born in Marlborough, Massachusetts (d. 1984)
Charley Toorop, Dutch painter (3 Generations), born in Katwijk, Netherlands (d. 1955)
John Knittel, Swiss writer, born in Dharwar, India (d. 1970)
Sergey Ivanovich Vavilov, Soviet physicist (Vavilov-Cherenkov effect), born in Moscow, Russian Empire (d. 1951)[1][2]
George Sisler, American Baseball HOF first baseman (AL MVP 1922; AL batting champion 1920, 22 St. Louis Browns) and manager (St. Louis Browns 1924–26), born in Manchester, Ohio (d. 1973)
Syd Saylor [Leo Sailor], American silent and sound screen character actor (Arizona Days; The Three Mesquiteers), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1962)
Gianna Manzini, Italian writer (Ritratto in piedi), born in Pistoia, Italy (d. 1974)
Charles Eyck, Dutch painter and sculptor (Limburg School), born in Meerssen, Netherlands (d. 1983)
Lucia Chase, American ballerina and co-founder of American Ballet Theater, born in Waterbury, Connecticut (d. 1986)
Wilhelm Reich, Austrian-American psychoanalyst (coined term "sexual revolution", worked with Freud), born in Dobzau, Austria-Hungary (d. 1957)
Dorothy Stratton, American coast guard officer (1st woman officer in US Coast Guard, SPARS), born in Brookfield, Missouri (d. 2006)
George Alpert, American railroad executive (New Haven Line), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1988)
(Algeria Junius) "June" Clark, American jazz trumpeter and cornetist, born in Long Branch, New Jersey (d. 1963)
Ub Iwerks, American animator and special-effects technician who worked on the design on Mickey Mouse, born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1971)[1][2]
Adolph Butenandt, German bio-chemist (Nobel Prize for Chemistry 1939 for work on sex hormones), born in Bremerhaven-Wesermünde, Germany (d. 1995)
Malcolm Muggeridge, English journalist, critic and spy (Observer of Life), born in Croydon, England (d. 1990)
Patrick Ludlow, British actor (Naughty Husbands, Evergreen and Modesty Blaise), born in Kensington, London, England (d. 1996)
Martin Kosleck, German actor (Pursuit to Algiers; Agent for H.A.R.M.), born in Barkotzen, Pomerania, Germany (d. 1994)
Pura Santillan Castrence, Filipino writer and diplomat (As I See It: Filipinos and the Philippines), born in Manila, Philippines (d. 2007)[1][2]
Klavdiya Shulzhenko, Soviet jazz and pop singer ("Let's Smoke"), born in Kharkiv, Russian Empire (now Ukraine) (d. 1984)
Janet Harmon Bragg, American pilot (1st female black aviator to hold a commercial pilot's license), born in Griffin, Georgia (d. 1993)[1]
Lauris Norstad, American general (NATO commander) and CEO (Owens-Corning Fiberglass), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 1988)
Lydia Chukovskaya, Soviet writer and dissident (Andrei Sakharov Prize for Writer's Civic Courage), born in Helsingfors, Duchy of Finland (d. 1996)
Paul Sauvé, Canadian lawyer, WWII veteran, and 17th Premier of Québec (1959-60), born in Saint-Benoît, Québec (d. 1960)[1]
Thomas "Tommy" Trinder, British radio comedian and actor (Save a Little Sunshine, Phoenix), born in Streatham, London, England (d. 1989)
Jacques Chailley, French musicologist and composer, born in Paris (d. 1999)
Richard Conte, American actor (The Godfather, Tony Rome, Hotel), born in Jersey City, New Jersey (d. 1975)
Herman W. "Fritz" Liebert, American rare books scholar and librarian (Yale University), born in New York City (d. 1994)
Jane Beverly Drew, English modernist architect, born in Croydon (d. 1996)
Paul Lorenzen, German philosopher and mathematician who co-founded Erlangen School of epistemological constructivism at University of Erlangen, Germany, born in Kiel, German Empire (d. 1994)
Donald Hamilton, American writer of novels, short stories, and non-fiction about the outdoors (The Big Country), born in Uppsala, Sweden (d. 2006)[1]
Constantin Andreou, Brazilian-Greek sculptor (Egg-Family) and founder of the Costas Andreou Foundation, born in São Paulo, Brazil (d. 2007)[1]
Ed Furgol, American golfer (US Open 1954), born in New York Mills, NY (d. 1997)
John Kendrew, British molecular biologist and winner of the Nobel Prize in Chemistry 1962, born in Oxford, England (d. 1997)[1]
Englebert van Anderlecht, Belgian abstract painter, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1961)
John J. Duncan Sr., American politician (Rep-R-TN, 1965-1988), born in Huntsville, Tennessee (d. 1988)
Lawrence Ferlinghetti, American beat poet (Coney Island of the Mind), born in Bronxville, New York (d. 2021)[1]
Robert Heilbroner, American economist and historian of economic thought (The Worldly Philosophers: The Lives, Times and Ideas of the Great Economic Thinkers), born in New York City (d. 2005)
Gene Nelson [Leander Berg], American screenwriter, director and actor (Tea For 2, Oklahoma), born in Seattle, Washington (d. 1996)
Sir Wilson Harris, Guyanese author (Palace of the Peacock:; Jonestown; The Ghost of Memory), born in New Amsterdam, British Guiana (d. 2018)
Vasily Smyslov, Russian chess grandmaster, 7th World Chess Champion (1957-8), born in Moscow, Russian SFSR (d. 2010)

Dave Appell, American musician, arranger, musical director (Ernie Kovacs), record producer (Charlie Gracie; The Orlons; Tony Orlando and Dawn), and songwriter, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2014)
King Pleasure [Clarence Beeks], American jazz singer and bebop vocalese master, born in Oakdale, Tennessee (d. 1982)
Onna White, Canadian choreographer (Oliver, The Music Man), born in Inverness, Canada (d. 2005)[1][2]
Murray Hamilton, American character actor (Rich Man Poor Man, Anatomy of a Murder), born in Washington, North Carolina (d. 1986)
"Boogie" Bill Webb, American Louisiana blues and R&B guitarist, singer and songwriter ("Bad Dog"), born in Jackson, Mississippi (d. 1990)
Eric Parkin, British classical pianist and champion of British composers, born in Stevenage, England (d. 2020)
Lois Andrews [Lorraine Gourley], American actress (Dixie Dugan, Meet Me After the Show, The Desert Hawk), born in Huntington Park, California (d. 1968)
Norman Fell, American actor (Three's Company, The End, Graduate), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1998)
Duncan Wood, English TV director and producer (Hancock's Half Hour, Steptoe & Son), born in Bristol, England (d. 1997)
Dario Fo, Italian playwright and performer (Nobel Prize Literature 1997), born in Sangiano (d. 2016)
Guillermo Timoner, Spanish cyclist (UCI Motor-paced World C'ships 1955, 59, 60, 62, 64, 65), born in Felanitx, Spain (d. 2023)
János Decsényi, Hungarian composer (Farewell to a Vanished Century), born in Budapest, Hungary
Byron Janis [né Yanks], American concert pianist (debut with NBC Symphony Orchestra at age 15), born in McKeesport, Pennsylvania (d. 2024)[1]
Vanessa Brown [Smylla Brind], Austrian-born American actress (My Favorite Husband), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1999)
Cuan McCarthy, South African cricketer (36 Test wkts for South Africa, 1 career no-ball), born in Pietermaritzburg, Union of South Africa (d. 2000)
Kenneth Nelson, American actor (Henry Aldrich-Aldrich Family), born in Rocky Mount, North Carolina (d. 1993)
Connie Hines, American actress (Mister Ed), born in Dedham, Massachusetts (d. 2009)
William Smith, American actor (Rich Man Poor Man; Any Which Way You Can; Hawaii 5-0), born in Columbia, Missouri (d. 2021)
